frameworks.name: deep neural network model of perceptual confidence
frameworks.one_line: DNN trained on naturalistic stimuli models confidence as probability-of-correct readout

frameworks.name: rational/normative account of confidence
frameworks.one_line: confidence biases follow from optimization to sensory input statistics

frameworks.name: common decision variable hypothesis
frameworks.one_line: decisions and confidence share an underlying decision variable despite dissociations

frameworks.name: low-dimensional idealized confidence models
frameworks.one_line: prior models relied on strong assumptions over abstract representations

core_question: Can decision-confidence dissociations be explained rationally by optimization to natural sensory statistics, while still sharing a decision variable with the choice itself?
key_assumptions: confidence is a prediction of probability-correct
perceptual system is optimized for high-dimensional naturalistic stimulus statistics
a single computational model can capture both decision and confidence readouts
manipulations: training on high-dimensional naturalistic stimuli vs. idealized low-dimensional inputs
stimulus regimes producing known decision-confidence dissociations
key_findings: DNN model reproduces puzzling dissociations between decisions and confidence
dissociations emerge as rational consequences of optimization to natural input statistics
decisions and confidence are driven by a common underlying decision variable in the model
interpretations: apparent suboptimality of confidence is a rational byproduct of natural-statistics-tuned representations
shared decision variable and observed dissociations are not mutually exclusive
